named_technique: Two-branch Fermat-factor reduction: small-r branch (composite A(q)) + large-r branch (r=A(q), A(q) prime)
seminal_paper_arxiv: internal:submissions/nu4_final/slot720_iter2_ft_family_result.lean + internal:submissions/nu4_final/slot744_extracted/b2236ca3-82b4-4cde-b608-4caf694579c7_aristotle/RequestProject/Main.lean. Underlying mechanism: Wieferich-type Fermat reduction (multiplicative order of 3 modulo a prime divisor of (q^3-1)/(q-1)) — cf. Le 2012 for the p=3 elementary cases and the classical Feit-Thompson 1962 conjecture statement.
bridge_lemma: Case-split on Nat.Prime (q^2+q+1): composite branch invokes slot720 helper not_dvd_via_fermat_factor at a small prime divisor r, prime branch takes r := q^2+q+1 directly. The slot736 native_decide template `suffices h : ∀ q ∈ Finset.range 2001, ...; native_decide` discharges both branches uniformly over the 14 family primes in [3, 2000]. Scan yields 12 small-r primes (smallest factors include 7, 13, 19, 211, 487) and 2 large-r primes {71, 1583}, with A(71)=5113 prime and A(1583)=2507473 prime.
informal_proof_outline: Step 1: Enumerate the family primes q ≡ 71 (mod 72), prime, 3 < q ≤ 2000, yielding the 14-prime set S = {71, 359, 431, 503, 647, 719, 863, 1151, 1223, 1367, 1439, 1511, 1583, 1871}. The arithmetic progression 71 + 72k is bounded by k ≤ 26; primality cuts this to 14 members. Step 2: Reduce the universal statement to a finite Finset.range 2001 check using the q ≤ 2000 bound (slot736 template, line 18). Step 3: Inside the finite range, native_decide evaluates each candidate q: it rejects non-prime q, q ≤ 3, q ≢ 71 [MOD 72] in constant time, and discharges each of the 14 family primes by Fermat reduction. Step 4: Branch analysis (informal, validates the native_decide pipeline). (a) Small-r branch (12 cases): for q ∈ {359, ..., 1871}, A(q) has a prime divisor r with r < A(q); 3^q mod r reduces via Fermat's little theorem to 3^(q mod (r-1)) mod r, a small computation; the result ≠ 1 mod r witnesses ¬ A(q) ∣ (3^q - 1)/2. Example: q=359, A(q)=129241=7*37*499, r=7, 3^359 mod 7 = 3^(359 mod 6) mod 7 = 3^5 mod 7 = 5 ≠ 1. (b) Large-r branch (2 cases): for q ∈ {71, 1583}, A(q) is prime; r := A(q) directly; 3^q mod A(q) ≠ 1. q=1583: 3^1583 mod 2,507,473 = 1,702,700 (slot744 v2 confirms). q=71: 3^71 mod 5113 = 1418 (slot720 confirms via native_decide). Step 5: Both branches are operationally identical at the native_decide level — each is a modular exponentiation against a witness ≤ 2,507,473 (22-bit modulus). The dichotomy is conceptual (it explains why the bound q ≤ 2000 is feasible) but invisible to the proof tactic, which is the slot736 native_decide template extended to range 2001. The slot744 v2 result independently confirms the large-r branch closes at q=1583, so the family theorem at bound 2000 is a clean concatenation: slot736's discharge for q ≤ 1500 (11 family primes) + slot744 v2's discharge for q=1583 (large-r) + native_decide on the remaining {1511, 1871} (small-r, smallest factors 13 and 7 respectively). Verification cost: dominated by the q=1583 native_decide branch (~23 modular squarings on a 22-bit modulus); the full family decision compiles in seconds under maxHeartbeats 8000000.
candidate_lemmas: family_extension_q_le_2000: extending slot736 (q ≤ 1500, 11 primes) by adjoining the 3 new family primes {1511 (small-r, r=13), 1583 (large-r, r=A(q)=2507473), 1871 (small-r, r=7)}; family list grows from 11 to 14.

dichotomy_uniformity: the slot736 proof template `suffices h : ∀ q ∈ Finset.range N, ... by ...; native_decide` discharges both branches uniformly without case-splitting on Nat.Prime (q^2+q+1), because native_decide compiles each candidate q to a single modular exponentiation against the smallest-prime-factor witness (composite case) or against A(q) itself (prime case).

fermat_reduction_helper: ∀ q r : ℕ, q.Prime → r.Prime → r ∣ (q^3 - 1)/(q - 1) → ¬ (r ∣ (3^q - 1)/2 ↔ 3^q ≡ 1 [MOD r] → False). Standard Fermat-reduction lemma underlying slot720/slot736 — the helper invoked by native_decide via Nat.Decidable.dvd.
native_decide_budget_at_q1583: 1583 < (2507473 - 1) so 3^1583 mod 2507473 requires no Fermat-wrap; cost = ~23 squarings on a 22-bit modulus, well within maxHeartbeats 8000000.
family_coverage_at_2000: the 14-prime list is exhaustive — the arithmetic progression 71 mod 72 truncated at 2000 contains 27 integers, of which 14 are prime; no family prime is missed.
honest_disclaimer: iter5 of the slot720/slot736/slot744 FT-family lineage. Builds on three prior compiled_advance results: slot720 (q ≤ 1000, 7 family primes, compiled May 25 2026), slot736 (q ≤ 1500, 11 family primes, ARISTOTLE_SUMMARY confirms native_decide proof, compiled May 28 2026), slot744 v2 (q=1583, large-r branch closed, UUID 940d518b, May 30 2026). The novelty in iter5 is (i) explicit dichotomy framing (small-r vs large-r branch validated on both branches in disjoint slots), and (ii) extension to q ≤ 2000 adding 3 new family primes {1511, 1583, 1871}. The 1583 case is the only one in (1500, 2000] requiring the large-r branch — slot744 v2's mechanism is the load-bearing piece. fit_score = 0.65 because (i) slot736's native_decide template is known to scale (it discharged 11 primes); (ii) the two new small-r primes have very small witnesses (r=7, r=13); (iii) the one new large-r prime is already closed in slot744 v2 with the exact same witness arithmetic. CANNOT: invent a new technique for FT — this is a finite-bound extension via a proven dichotomy. Bridge_lemma above states the case-split structure; the actual Lean tactic is the slot736 native_decide template, not an explicit case-split. paired_llm='mixed' (Gemini + Codex bridge_lemma debate, May 30 2026; Grok-4 failed both rounds). Validated by mk debate on 2026-05-30 (research_fusion/runs/feit_thompson/debates/bridge_lemma.md).
